   207→   207→### Security Warning
   208→   208→
   209→   209→> **⚠️ DANGEROUS PERMISSIONS REQUIRED:** Running Ralph requires `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, which grants unrestricted system access.
   210→   210→
   211→   211→**What This Flag Enables:**
   212→   212→
   213→   213→- Arbitrary code execution without approval
   214→   214→- Full filesystem access (including SSH keys, cookies, credentials)
   215→   215→- Unrestricted network access
   216→   216→- Environment variable exposure
   217→   217→
   218→   218→**Mandatory Mitigations:**
   219→   219→
   220→   220→1. Always run in sandbox (Docker, VM, or macOS Seatbelt/Linux Landlock)
   221→   221→2. Use dedicated API keys with spend limits
   222→   222→3. Never run on production systems
   223→   223→4. Audit git history after each session
   224→   224→5. Implement Principal Skinner pattern for production
   225→   225→
   226→   226→See: [Security Considerations](#security-considerations), [The Principal Skinner Pattern](#the-principal-skinner-pattern)
   227→   227→
   228→   228→---
   229→   229→
   230→   230→### Cost Disclaimer
   231→   231→
   232→   232→> **⚠️ UNVERIFIED CLAIMS:** Results like "$50K contract for $297" are anecdotal, self-reported, and unverified.
   233→   233→
   234→   234→Actual costs vary dramatically based on:
   235→   235→
   236→   236→- Task complexity and scope
   237→   237→- Context window utilization per iteration
   238→   238→- Model selection (Opus vs. Sonnet)
   239→   239→- Validation failure rates
   240→   240→- MCP server overhead
   241→   241→
   242→   242→See [Cost Modeling](#cost-modeling) for estimation formulas with confidence intervals.
   243→   243→
   244→   244→---
   245→   245→
   246→   246→## Origin Story
   247→   247→
   248→   248→### The Creator: Geoffrey Huntley
   249→   249→
   250→   250→The Ralph Wiggum technique was created by **Geoffrey Huntley**, an Australian open-source developer and goat farmer, in **May 2025**. He formalized it in a blog post on **July 14, 2025**.
   251→   251→
   252→   252→**Timeline:**
   253→   253→| Date | Event |
   254→   254→|------|-------|
   255→   255→| May 2025 | Initial technique development |
   256→   256→| June 2025 | Dex Horthy (HumanLayer) begins experimenting with Ralph |
   257→   257→| July 14, 2025 | Huntley publishes [ghuntley.com/ralph/](https://ghuntley.com/ralph/) |
   258→   258→| December 2025 | Technique goes viral; Boris Cherny launches official plugin |
   259→   259→| January 13, 2026 | Dev Interrupted podcast; mainstream adoption |
   260→   260→| January 17, 2026 | Huntley demonstrates "evolutionary software auto heal" |
   261→   261→
   262→   262→Huntley was frustrated by a fundamental limitation in agentic AI coding workflows: the **"human-in-the-loop" bottleneck**. While AI models were capable, they were constrained by users manually reviewing and re-prompting after every error.
   263→   263→
   264→   264→The discovery had a visceral impact:
   265→   265→
   266→   266→> "It literally made me wanna Ralph [vomit]... I was feeling really sick in the stomach, knowing what I essentially discovered... I walk around, I see dead people like the sixth sense. Like it's not like they're literally dead, just, they just don't know that they don't have a job yet."
   267→   267→> — Geoffrey Huntley, [Dev Interrupted Podcast](https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/inventing-the-ralph-wiggum-loop) (January 2026)
   268→   268→
   269→   269→### The Five-Line Script That Changed Everything
   270→   270→
   271→   271→His solution was elegantly simple:
   272→   272→
   273→   273→```bash
   274→   274→while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done
   275→   275→```
   276→   276→
   277→   277→This infinite loop continuously feeds a prompt file to Claude Code, iterating until desired results emerge.
   278→   278→
   279→   279→**Key Properties:**
   280→   280→
   281→   281→- **Stateless:** Each iteration spawns a fresh Claude instance
   282→   282→- **File-based memory:** State persists only through git, AGENTS.md, and task files
   283→   283→- **Deterministic loop:** Same input → reproducible behavior
   284→   284→- **Failure-tolerant:** Errors become input for next iteration
   285→   285→
   286→   286→### Why "Ralph Wiggum"?
   287→   287→
   288→   288→The technique is named after **Ralph Wiggum** from _The Simpsons_—the dim-witted but relentlessly optimistic character who never gives up despite repeated failures.
   289→   289→
   290→   290→> "Naive persistence" and "brute-force repetition" rather than elegant optimization.
   291→   291→
   292→   292→As Huntley puts it:
   293→   293→
   294→   294→> "That's the beauty of Ralph—the technique is deterministically bad in an undeterministic world."
   295→   295→
   296→   296→### The CURSED Language: A Three-Month Case Study
   297→   297→
   298→   298→Huntley validated Ralph by building an entire programming language—CURSED (a Gen Z slang-based language)—over three months using only Ralph loops:
   299→   299→
   300→   300→- Stage-2 compiler
   301→   301→- Standard library
   302→   302→- Implementations in C, Rust, and Zig
   303→   303→- LLVM backend
   304→   304→
   305→   305→This demonstrated that Ralph could handle sustained, complex development work far beyond simple scripts.
   306→   306→
   307→   307→**Source:** [ghuntley.com/cursed/](https://ghuntley.com/cursed/)
   308→   308→
   309→   309→### Adoption by Anthropic
   310→   310→
   311→   311→After observing Ralph's impact, **Boris Cherny** (Head of Claude Code at Anthropic) officially incorporated Huntley's approach into the **ralph-wiggum plugin** in December 2025.
   312→   312→
   313→   313→> **⚠️ Critical:** Anthropic's implementation differs fundamentally from Huntley's original philosophy. See [The Two Ralphs](#the-two-ralphs-huntley-vs-anthropic).
   314→   314→
   315→   315→---
   316→   316→
   317→   317→## The Two Ralphs: Huntley vs. Anthropic
   318→   318→
   319→   319→> **This section addresses the most important architectural decision in Ralph deployment. The choice between these approaches materially affects results.**
   320→   320→
   321→   321→### The Core Contradiction
   322→   322→
   323→   323→| Aspect                  | Huntley's Original                | Anthropic's Plugin                |
   324→   324→| ----------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
   325→   325→| **Context Model**       | Fresh context per iteration       | Single persistent context         |
   326→   326→| **Memory**              | Files only (git, AGENTS.md)       | In-context + compaction           |
   327→   327→| **Architecture**        | Spawns NEW Claude instance        | Stop Hook within SAME session     |
   328→   328→| **Failure Handling**    | "Naive persistence" - raw errors  | Structured failure formatting     |
   329→   329→| **Safety**              | Minimal - operator responsibility | Built-in limits, hooks            |
   330→   330→| **Session Isolation**   | Complete                          | Can bleed across sessions         |
   331→   331→| **Context Degradation** | Impossible (always fresh)         | Progressive (requires compaction) |
   332→   332→
   333→   333→### Huntley's Philosophy: Fresh Context is Non-Negotiable
   334→   334→
   335→   335→Geoffrey Huntley's core insight is that **each iteration must spawn a completely fresh context**:
   336→   336→
   337→   337→> "One context window, one activity, one goal."
   338→   338→> — [The Real Ralph Wiggum Loop](https://thetrav.substack.com/p/the-real-ralph-wiggum-loop-what-everyone)
   339→   339→
   340→   340→**Why this matters:**
   341→   341→
   342→   342→```
   343→   343→HUNTLEY'S APPROACH (The Real Thing):
   344→   344→┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
   345→   345→│ Iteration 1 │ ──► │ Iteration 2 │ ──► │ Iteration 3 │
   346→   346→│ FRESH       │     │ FRESH       │     │ FRESH       │
   347→   347→│ CONTEXT     │     │ CONTEXT     │     │ CONTEXT     │
   348→   348→└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘
   349→   349→       │                   │                   │
   350→   350→       ▼                   ▼                   ▼
   351→   351→    [FILES]             [FILES]             [FILES]
   352→   352→    git, AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
   353→   353→    (Memory persists ONLY through filesystem)
   354→   354→```
   355→   355→
   356→   356→**Cannot suffer context degradation** because each iteration starts clean.
   357→   357→
   358→   358→### Anthropic's Plugin: A Different Architecture
   359→   359→
   360→   360→Anthropic's implementation uses a **Stop Hook within a single persistent context**:
   361→   361→
   362→   362→```
   363→   363→ANTHROPIC'S APPROACH (Different Architecture):
   364→   364→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   365→   365→│              SINGLE PERSISTENT CONTEXT              │
   366→   366→│                                                     │
   367→   367→│  Task 1 ──► Task 2 ──► Task 3 ──► COMPACTION ──►   │
   368→   368→│                                                     │
   369→   369→│  Context grows... degrades... compacts... loses     │
   370→   370→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   371→   371→```
   372→   372→
   373→   373→### The "Dumb Zone" Problem
   374→   374→
   375→   375→Research by Dex Horthy (HumanLayer) and academic studies from Stanford/UC Berkeley identify the **"Dumb Zone"**—the context utilization range (approximately 40-60%) where LLM reasoning quality noticeably degrades.
   376→   376→
   377→   377→**The "Lost in the Middle" Phenomenon:**
   378→   378→
   379→   379→- LLMs pay less attention to information in the middle of long contexts
   380→   380→- Recall and reasoning degrade as context fills
   381→   381→- Quality degradation is not linear—it accelerates
   382→   382→
   383→   383→**Huntley's approach avoids this entirely** by never letting context accumulate. Anthropic's approach requires careful compaction to stay in the "Smart Zone" (below ~40%).
   384→   384→
   385→   385→### Community Criticism of Anthropic's Plugin
   386→   386→
   387→   387→> "It dies in cryptic ways unless you have --dangerously-skip-permissions. It installs hooks in weird places you can't find, uses a strange markdown file to track state, and adds an opaque stop hook for all claude sessions in a directory until you disable it."
   388→   388→> — [HumanLayer Blog](https://www.humanlayer.dev/blog/brief-history-of-ralph)
   389→   389→
   390→   390→> "Beyond that, **it misses the key point of ralph which is not 'run forever' but in 'carve off small bits of work into independent context windows'.** I look forward to see more from Anthropic on this in the future, but for now I'll keep my 5-line bash loops."
   391→   391→> — Dex Horthy, HumanLayer
   392→   392→
   393→   393→### Recommendation: Use Huntley's Approach
   394→   394→
   395→   395→**For maximum effectiveness, follow Huntley's original pattern:**
   396→   396→
   397→   397→```bash
   398→   398→# The Real Ralph - Fresh context per iteration
   399→   399→while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done
   400→   400→```
   401→   401→
   402→   402→**Key principles:**
   403→   403→
   404→   404→1. Each iteration = new Claude instance = fresh context
   405→   405→2. Memory persists ONLY through files (git, AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md)
   406→   406→3. "One context window, one activity, one goal"
   407→   407→4. Let the AI confront its own failures without sanitization
   408→   408→
   409→   409→**When to consider Anthropic's plugin:**
   410→   410→
   411→   411→- Enterprise environments requiring strict safety controls
   412→   412→- When you need built-in iteration limits for cost management
   413→   413→- When you can't manage the bash loop yourself
   414→   414→- **Note:** Plugin is currently broken; use workarounds
   415→   415→
   416→   416→---
   417→   417→
   418→   418→## Philosophy & Theoretical Basis
   419→   419→
   420→   420→### Core Principles
   421→   421→
   422→   422→The Ralph methodology rests on four foundational principles:
   423→   423→
   424→   424→#### 1. Context Scarcity
   425→   425→
   426→   426→With ~176K usable tokens from a 200K context window (after system prompts, MCP servers, and AGENTS.md), bloated prompts waste capacity.
   427→   427→
   428→   428→> "Find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that maximize the likelihood of your desired outcome."
   429→   429→> — [Anthropic Engineering Blog](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents)
   430→   430→
   431→   431→**Practical Implication:** Keep iterations lean. Don't dump entire codebases into context.
   432→   432→
   433→   433→#### 2. Plan Disposability
   434→   434→
   435→   435→A drifting plan regenerates cheaper than salvaging stale state. Treat plans as coordination scaffolding, not binding contracts.
   436→   436→
   437→   437→> "Stale plans should be regenerated rather than salvaged."
   438→   438→> — [The Ralph Wiggum Playbook](https://paddo.dev/blog/ralph-wiggum-playbook/)
   439→   439→
   440→   440→**Practical Implication:** If your IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md drifts significantly from reality, regenerate it rather than trying to patch it.
   441→   441→
   442→   442→#### 3. Backpressure Over Direction
   443→   443→
   444→   444→Engineer environments where incorrect outputs get rejected automatically rather than explicitly instructing agents what to do.
   445→   445→
   446→   446→> "Human roles change from directing the agent to engineering conditions where good outcomes emerge naturally."
   447→   447→
   448→   448→**Practical Implication:** Tests, linters, and type checkers should fail fast and loud.
   449→   449→
   450→   450→#### 4. Failure as Feedback
   451→   451→
   452→   452→Errors are not bugs to be avoided—they are information that guides the next iteration.
   453→   453→
   454→   454→> "Deterministically bad means failures are predictable and informative. Use them to tune prompts."
   455→   455→> — Geoffrey Huntley
   456→   456→
   457→   457→### The Paradigm Shift
   458→   458→
   459→   459→Huntley argues that software development has fundamentally shifted:
   460→   460→
   461→   461→> "Software is now clay on the pottery wheel and if something isn't right then I just throw it back on the wheel."
   462→   462→> — [Everything is a Ralph Loop](https://ghuntley.com/loop/)
   463→   463→
   464→   464→| Old Paradigm             | New Paradigm           |
   465→   465→| ------------------------ | ---------------------- |
   466→   466→| Waterfall planning       | True Agile for AI      |
   467→   467→| Prescriptive instruction | Environmental steering |
   468→   468→| Manual review cycles     | Automated backpressure |
   469→   469→| Code as artifact         | Code as clay           |
   470→   470→| Developer as coder       | Developer as conductor |
   471→   471→
   472→   472→### Context Engineering vs. Prompt Engineering
   473→   473→
   474→   474→The term **"Context Engineering"** emerged from multiple contributors in mid-2025:
   475→   475→
   476→   476→| Date               | Contributor               | Contribution                                                                                                    |
   477→   477→| ------------------ | ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   478→   478→| April 2025         | Dex Horthy                | [12-Factor Agents](https://github.com/humanlayer/12-factor-agents) framework                                    |
   479→   479→| June 12, 2025      | Walden Yan (Cognition AI) | Foundational blog post                                                                                          |
   480→   480→| June 19, 2025      | Tobi Lütke (Shopify CEO)  | Public endorsement of term                                                                                      |
   481→   481→| June 23, 2025      | LangChain                 | [Rise of Context Engineering](https://www.blog.langchain.com/the-rise-of-context-engineering/)                  |
   482→   482→| June 25, 2025      | Andrej Karpathy           | Influential "+1" endorsement                                                                                    |
   483→   483→| September 29, 2025 | Anthropic                 | [Official engineering guide](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents) |
   484→   484→
   485→   485→**Definition (Anthropic):**
   486→   486→
   487→   487→> "Context Engineering is building dynamic systems to provide the right information and tools in the right format such that the LLM can plausibly accomplish the task."
   488→   488→
   489→   489→| Prompt Engineering      | Context Engineering            |
   490→   490→| ----------------------- | ------------------------------ |
   491→   491→| Finding the right words | Curating the right information |
   492→   492→| Static instruction      | Dynamic state management       |
   493→   493→| Single-turn focus       | Multi-turn lifecycle           |
   494→   494→| Prompt composition      | Holistic context state         |
   495→   495→| Trial and error         | System architecture            |
   496→   496→
   497→   497→---
   498→   498→
   499→   499→## How It Works
   500→   500→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

    97→
    98→## Important Notices (January 2026)
    99→
   100→### Critical: Plugin Status
   101→
   102→> **⚠️ PLUGIN BROKEN:** Anthropic's official ralph-wiggum plugin is currently non-functional due to security patch side effects.
   103→
   104→**Technical Details:**
   105→
   106→- **CVE:** [CVE-2025-54795](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-54795)
   107→- **CVSS Score:** 8.7 (High)
   108→- **CWE:** CWE-78 (Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command)
   109→- **Disclosure Date:** August 4, 2025
   110→- **Discoverer:** Elad Beber, Cymulate
   111→- **Fix Version:** Claude Code v1.0.20
   112→
   113→**What Happened:** The vulnerability allowed command injection via the `echo` command, bypassing confirmation prompts. Anthropic's fix correctly blocks `$()` command substitution and multi-line bash commands. However, the official Ralph plugin's command file _relies on_ multi-line bash—so the security fix breaks the plugin.
   114→
   115→**Error Message:**
   116→
   117→```
   118→Bash command permission check failed for pattern "...":
   119→Command contains newlines that could separate multiple commands
   120→```
   121→
   122→**Workarounds:**
   123→
   124→1. **Huntley's original bash loop** (recommended):
   125→   ```bash
   126→   while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done
   127→   ```
   128→2. **dial481/ralph** - Reimplementation using Claude's Write tool instead of bash execution: [github.com/dial481/ralph](https://github.com/dial481/ralph)
   129→
   130→**Source:** [Cymulate Blog](https://cymulate.com/blog/cve-2025-547954-54795-claude-inverseprompt/), [GitHub Advisory GHSA-x56v-x2h6-7j34](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/security/advisories/GHSA-x56v-x2h6-7j34)
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→### Critical: Anthropic Crackdown
   135→
   136→> **⚠️ THIRD-PARTY ACCESS BLOCKED:** As of January 9, 2026 (02:20 UTC), Anthropic deployed technical safeguards blocking third-party tools from using Claude Pro/Max subscription OAuth tokens.
   137→
   138→**What Was Blocked:**
   139→
   140→- OpenCode (56K GitHub stars)
   141→- Cursor users accessing Claude via OAuth
   142→- Any tool spoofing Claude Code client headers
   143→- xAI employees (separate enforcement action)
   144→
   145→**Error Message:**
   146→
   147→```
   148→This credential is only authorized for use with Claude Code.
   149→```
   150→
   151→**Economic Context:** A $200/month Max subscription provided unlimited tokens through Claude Code. The same usage via API would cost $1,000+/month. Third-party tools removed Claude Code's rate limits, enabling overnight autonomous loops at consumer prices. Anthropic closed this "subscription arbitrage."
   152→
   153→**Community Response:**
   154→
   155→- DHH (Ruby on Rails creator): "very customer hostile"
   156→- Mass subscription cancellations reported
   157→- OpenCode shipped ChatGPT Plus support within hours
   158→
   159→**Official Statement:** Thariq Shihipar (Anthropic): "Yesterday we tightened our safeguards against spoofing the Claude Code harness after accounts were banned for triggering abuse filters from third-party harnesses."
   160→
   161→**Source:** [paddo.dev](https://paddo.dev/blog/anthropic-walled-garden-crackdown/), [VentureBeat](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-cracks-down-on-unauthorized-claude-usage-by-third-party-harnesses)
   162→
   163→---
   164→
   165→### Claude Code 2.1.12 (January 21, 2026) - Latest
   166→
   167→> **✨ CURRENT VERSION:** Claude Code 2.1.12 is the latest stable release as of January 21, 2026.
   168→
   169→**Key Updates Since 2.1.0:**
   170→
   171→- Pro users now have access to **Opus 4.5** as part of their subscription
   172→- Customizable keyboard shortcuts via `~/.claude/keybindings.json`
   173→- `showTurnDuration` setting to hide turn duration messages
   174→- Feedback option on permission prompts
   175→- Inline agent response display in task notifications
   176→- **Security fix:** Wildcard permission rules no longer match compound commands with shell operators
   177→- MCP tool search auto mode enabled by default
   178→- Setup hook for `--init` and related flags
   179→- Fixed crashes with heredocs containing JavaScript template literals
   180→- Deprecation notification for npm installations (use `claude install` instead)
   181→
   182→**Source:** [npm @anthropic-ai/claude-code](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@anthropic-ai/claude-code), [Claude Code Changelog](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)
   183→
   184→---
   185→
   186→### Active Issues (January 2026)
   187→
   188→> **Note:** The following issue numbers are from community tracking and reports.
   189→
   190→| Issue                    | Description                                                                     | Date    | Status               |
   191→| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- | -------------------- |
   192→| Multi-line permission    | Permission check fails on multi-line scripts                                    | Jan 2   | Open                 |
   193→| Slash command permission | Slash command fails permission check                                            | Jan 5   | Open                 |
   194→| Max-iterations syntax    | `--max-iterations=X` syntax ignored; use `--max-iterations X` (space-separated) | Jan 16  | Confirmed            |

   196→| TaskMaster JSON parsing  | Claude Code API error during object generation with task-master-ai              | Ongoing | Workaround available |
   197→
   198→**Mitigation for Session Bleed:**
   199→
   200→1. Run `/cancel-ralph` in affected directories
   201→2. Delete `.claude/ralph-loop.local.md` if present
   202→3. Disable plugin entirely: `/plugin disable ralph-wiggum`
   203→4. Use Huntley's bash loop instead (inherently isolated)
   204→

   206→
   207→### Security Warning
   208→
   209→> **⚠️ DANGEROUS PERMISSIONS REQUIRED:** Running Ralph requires `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, which grants unrestricted system access.
   210→
   211→**What This Flag Enables:**
   212→
   213→- Arbitrary code execution without approval
   214→- Full filesystem access (including SSH keys, cookies, credentials)
   215→- Unrestricted network access
   216→- Environment variable exposure
   217→
   218→**Mandatory Mitigations:**
   219→
   220→1. Always run in sandbox (Docker, VM, or macOS Seatbelt/Linux Landlock)
   221→2. Use dedicated API keys with spend limits
   222→3. Never run on production systems
   223→4. Audit git history after each session
   224→5. Implement Principal Skinner pattern for production
   225→
   226→See: [Security Considerations](#security-considerations), [The Principal Skinner Pattern](#the-principal-skinner-pattern)

   234→Actual costs vary dramatically based on:

   236→- Task complexity and scope

   244→---
   245→
   246→## Origin Story
   247→
   248→### The Creator: Geoffrey Huntley
   249→
   250→The Ralph Wiggum technique was created by **Geoffrey Huntley**, an Australian open-source developer and goat farmer, in **May 2025**. He formalized it in a blog post on **July 14, 2025**.
   251→
   252→**Timeline:**
   253→| Date | Event |
   254→|------|-------|
   255→| May 2025 | Initial technique development |
   256→| June 2025 | Dex Horthy (HumanLayer) begins experimenting with Ralph |
   257→| July 14, 2025 | Huntley publishes [ghuntley.com/ralph/](https://ghuntley.com/ralph/) |
   258→| December 2025 | Technique goes viral; Boris Cherny launches official plugin |
   259→| January 13, 2026 | Dev Interrupted podcast; mainstream adoption |
   260→| January 17, 2026 | Huntley demonstrates "evolutionary software auto heal" |
   261→
   262→Huntley was frustrated by a fundamental limitation in agentic AI coding workflows: the **"human-in-the-loop" bottleneck**. While AI models were capable, they were constrained by users manually reviewing and re-prompting after every error.
   263→
   264→The discovery had a visceral impact:
   265→
   266→> "It literally made me wanna Ralph [vomit]... I was feeling really sick in the stomach, knowing what I essentially discovered... I walk around, I see dead people like the sixth sense. Like it's not like they're literally dead, just, they just don't know that they don't have a job yet."
   267→> — Geoffrey Huntley, [Dev Interrupted Podcast](https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/inventing-the-ralph-wiggum-loop) (January 2026)
   268→
   269→### The Five-Line Script That Changed Everything
   270→
   271→His solution was elegantly simple:
   272→
   273→```bash
   274→while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done
   275→```
   276→
   277→This infinite loop continuously feeds a prompt file to Claude Code, iterating until desired results emerge.
   278→
   279→**Key Properties:**
   280→
   281→- **Stateless:** Each iteration spawns a fresh Claude instance
   282→- **File-based memory:** State persists only through git, AGENTS.md, and task files
   283→- **Deterministic loop:** Same input → reproducible behavior
   284→- **Failure-tolerant:** Errors become input for next iteration
   285→
   286→### Why "Ralph Wiggum"?
   287→
   288→The technique is named after **Ralph Wiggum** from _The Simpsons_—the dim-witted but relentlessly optimistic character who never gives up despite repeated failures.
   289→
   290→> "Naive persistence" and "brute-force repetition" rather than elegant optimization.
   291→
   292→As Huntley puts it:
   293→
   294→> "That's the beauty of Ralph—the technique is deterministically bad in an undeterministic world."
   295→
   296→### The CURSED Language: A Three-Month Case Study
   297→
   298→Huntley validated Ralph by building an entire programming language—CURSED (a Gen Z slang-based language)—over three months using only Ralph loops:
   299→
   300→- Stage-2 compiler
   301→- Standard library
   302→- Implementations in C, Rust, and Zig
   303→- LLVM backend
   304→
   305→This demonstrated that Ralph could handle sustained, complex development work far beyond simple scripts.
   306→
   307→**Source:** [ghuntley.com/cursed/](https://ghuntley.com/cursed/)
   308→
   309→### Adoption by Anthropic
   310→
   311→After observing Ralph's impact, **Boris Cherny** (Head of Claude Code at Anthropic) officially incorporated Huntley's approach into the **ralph-wiggum plugin** in December 2025.
   312→
   313→> **⚠️ Critical:** Anthropic's implementation differs fundamentally from Huntley's original philosophy. See [The Two Ralphs](#the-two-ralphs-huntley-vs-anthropic).
   314→
   315→---
   316→
   317→## The Two Ralphs: Huntley vs. Anthropic
   318→
   319→> **This section addresses the most important architectural decision in Ralph deployment. The choice between these approaches materially affects results.**
   320→
   321→### The Core Contradiction
   322→
   323→| Aspect                  | Huntley's Original                | Anthropic's Plugin                |
   324→| ----------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
   325→| **Context Model**       | Fresh context per iteration       | Single persistent context         |
   326→| **Memory**              | Files only (git, AGENTS.md)       | In-context + compaction           |
   327→| **Architecture**        | Spawns NEW Claude instance        | Stop Hook within SAME session     |
   328→| **Failure Handling**    | "Naive persistence" - raw errors  | Structured failure formatting     |
   329→| **Safety**              | Minimal - operator responsibility | Built-in limits, hooks            |
   330→| **Session Isolation**   | Complete                          | Can bleed across sessions         |
   331→| **Context Degradation** | Impossible (always fresh)         | Progressive (requires compaction) |
   332→
   333→### Huntley's Philosophy: Fresh Context is Non-Negotiable
   334→
   335→Geoffrey Huntley's core insight is that **each iteration must spawn a completely fresh context**:
   336→
   337→> "One context window, one activity, one goal."
   338→> — [The Real Ralph Wiggum Loop](https://thetrav.substack.com/p/the-real-ralph-wiggum-loop-what-everyone)
   339→
   340→**Why this matters:**
   341→
   342→```
   343→HUNTLEY'S APPROACH (The Real Thing):
   344→┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
   345→│ Iteration 1 │ ──► │ Iteration 2 │ ──► │ Iteration 3 │
   346→│ FRESH       │     │ FRESH       │     │ FRESH       │
   347→│ CONTEXT     │     │ CONTEXT     │     │ CONTEXT     │
   348→└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘
   349→       │                   │                   │
   350→       ▼                   ▼                   ▼
   351→    [FILES]             [FILES]             [FILES]
   352→    git, AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md
   353→    (Memory persists ONLY through filesystem)
   354→```
   355→
   356→**Cannot suffer context degradation** because each iteration starts clean.
   357→
   358→### Anthropic's Plugin: A Different Architecture
   359→
   360→Anthropic's implementation uses a **Stop Hook within a single persistent context**:
   361→
   362→```
   363→ANTHROPIC'S APPROACH (Different Architecture):
   364→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   365→│              SINGLE PERSISTENT CONTEXT              │
   366→│                                                     │
   367→│  Task 1 ──► Task 2 ──► Task 3 ──► COMPACTION ──►   │
   368→│                                                     │
   369→│  Context grows... degrades... compacts... loses     │
   370→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   371→```
   372→
   373→### The "Dumb Zone" Problem
   374→
   375→Research by Dex Horthy (HumanLayer) and academic studies from Stanford/UC Berkeley identify the **"Dumb Zone"**—the context utilization range (approximately 40-60%) where LLM reasoning quality noticeably degrades.
   376→
   377→**The "Lost in the Middle" Phenomenon:**
   378→
   379→- LLMs pay less attention to information in the middle of long contexts
   380→- Recall and reasoning degrade as context fills
   381→- Quality degradation is not linear—it accelerates
   382→
   383→**Huntley's approach avoids this entirely** by never letting context accumulate. Anthropic's approach requires careful compaction to stay in the "Smart Zone" (below ~40%).
   384→
   385→### Community Criticism of Anthropic's Plugin
   386→
   387→> "It dies in cryptic ways unless you have --dangerously-skip-permissions. It installs hooks in weird places you can't find, uses a strange markdown file to track state, and adds an opaque stop hook for all claude sessions in a directory until you disable it."
   388→> — [HumanLayer Blog](https://www.humanlayer.dev/blog/brief-history-of-ralph)
   389→
   390→> "Beyond that, **it misses the key point of ralph which is not 'run forever' but in 'carve off small bits of work into independent context windows'.** I look forward to see more from Anthropic on this in the future, but for now I'll keep my 5-line bash loops."
   391→> — Dex Horthy, HumanLayer
   392→
   393→### Recommendation: Use Huntley's Approach
   394→
   395→**For maximum effectiveness, follow Huntley's original pattern:**
   396→
   397→```bash
   398→# The Real Ralph - Fresh context per iteration
   399→while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done
   400→```
   401→
   402→**Key principles:**
   403→
   404→1. Each iteration = new Claude instance = fresh context
   405→2. Memory persists ONLY through files (git, AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md)
   406→3. "One context window, one activity, one goal"
   407→4. Let the AI confront its own failures without sanitization
   408→
   409→**When to consider Anthropic's plugin:**
   410→
   411→- Enterprise environments requiring strict safety controls
   412→- When you need built-in iteration limits for cost management
   413→- When you can't manage the bash loop yourself
   414→- **Note:** Plugin is currently broken; use workarounds
   415→
   416→---
   417→
   418→## Philosophy & Theoretical Basis
   419→
   420→### Core Principles
   421→
   422→The Ralph methodology rests on four foundational principles:
   423→
   424→#### 1. Context Scarcity
   425→
   426→With ~176K usable tokens from a 200K context window (after system prompts, MCP servers, and AGENTS.md), bloated prompts waste capacity.
   427→
   428→> "Find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that maximize the likelihood of your desired outcome."
   429→> — [Anthropic Engineering Blog](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents)
   430→
   431→**Practical Implication:** Keep iterations lean. Don't dump entire codebases into context.
   432→
   433→#### 2. Plan Disposability
   434→
   435→A drifting plan regenerates cheaper than salvaging stale state. Treat plans as coordination scaffolding, not binding contracts.
   436→
   437→> "Stale plans should be regenerated rather than salvaged."
   438→> — [The Ralph Wiggum Playbook](https://paddo.dev/blog/ralph-wiggum-playbook/)
   439→
   440→**Practical Implication:** If your IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md drifts significantly from reality, regenerate it rather than trying to patch it.
   441→
   442→#### 3. Backpressure Over Direction
   443→
   444→Engineer environments where incorrect outputs get rejected automatically rather than explicitly instructing agents what to do.
   445→
   446→> "Human roles change from directing the agent to engineering conditions where good outcomes emerge naturally."
   447→
   448→**Practical Implication:** Tests, linters, and type checkers should fail fast and loud.
   449→
   450→#### 4. Failure as Feedback
   451→
   452→Errors are not bugs to be avoided—they are information that guides the next iteration.
   453→
   454→> "Deterministically bad means failures are predictable and informative. Use them to tune prompts."
   455→> — Geoffrey Huntley
   456→
   457→### The Paradigm Shift
   458→
   459→Huntley argues that software development has fundamentally shifted:
   460→
   461→> "Software is now clay on the pottery wheel and if something isn't right then I just throw it back on the wheel."
   462→> — [Everything is a Ralph Loop](https://ghuntley.com/loop/)
   463→
   464→| Old Paradigm             | New Paradigm           |
   465→| ------------------------ | ---------------------- |
   466→| Waterfall planning       | True Agile for AI      |
   467→| Prescriptive instruction | Environmental steering |
   468→| Manual review cycles     | Automated backpressure |
   469→| Code as artifact         | Code as clay           |
   470→| Developer as coder       | Developer as conductor |
   471→
   472→### Context Engineering vs. Prompt Engineering
   473→
   474→The term **"Context Engineering"** emerged from multiple contributors in mid-2025:
   475→
   476→| Date               | Contributor               | Contribution                                                                                                    |
   477→| ------------------ | ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   478→| April 2025         | Dex Horthy                | [12-Factor Agents](https://github.com/humanlayer/12-factor-agents) framework                                    |
   479→| June 12, 2025      | Walden Yan (Cognition AI) | Foundational blog post                                                                                          |
   480→| June 19, 2025      | Tobi Lütke (Shopify CEO)  | Public endorsement of term                                                                                      |
   481→| June 23, 2025      | LangChain                 | [Rise of Context Engineering](https://www.blog.langchain.com/the-rise-of-context-engineering/)                  |
   482→| June 25, 2025      | Andrej Karpathy           | Influential "+1" endorsement                                                                                    |
   483→| September 29, 2025 | Anthropic                 | [Official engineering guide](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents) |
   484→
   485→**Definition (Anthropic):**
   486→
   487→> "Context Engineering is building dynamic systems to provide the right information and tools in the right format such that the LLM can plausibly accomplish the task."
   488→
   489→| Prompt Engineering      | Context Engineering            |
   490→| ----------------------- | ------------------------------ |
   491→| Finding the right words | Curating the right information |
   492→| Static instruction      | Dynamic state management       |
   493→| Single-turn focus       | Multi-turn lifecycle           |
   494→| Prompt composition      | Holistic context state         |
   495→| Trial and error         | System architecture            |
   496→
   497→---
   498→
   499→## How It Works
   500→

content.content:
500→   500→
   501→   501→### The Core Loop
   502→   502→
   503→   503→Ralph operates through a self-sustaining cycle:
   504→   504→
   505→   505→```
   506→   506→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   507→   507→│                   RALPH LOOP                    │
   508→   508→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   509→   509→│  1. PARSE: Break task into actionable steps     │
   510→   510→│  2. INVOKE: Prompt AI with current state        │
   511→   511→│  3. EXECUTE: Run generated code (git-tracked)   │
   512→   512→│  4. ASSESS: Evaluate against completion criteria│
   513→   513→│  5. ITERATE: If incomplete, return to step 2    │
   514→   514→│  6. EXIT: Output completion promise             │
   515→   515→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   516→   516→```
   517→   517→
   518→   518→### Fresh Context Philosophy
   519→   519→
   520→   520→Each iteration spawns a **new AI instance with clean context**. Memory persists exclusively through:
   521→   521→
   522→   522→- Git history
   523→   523→- Modified files
   524→   524→- Progress files (e.g., `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md`)
   525→   525→- AGENTS.md discoveries
   526→   526→
   527→   527→This avoids the "Dumb Zone"—reasoning degradation that occurs around 40% context utilization.
   528→   528→
   529→   529→### Completion Signals
   530→   530→
   531→   531→Ralph terminates when a specific **completion promise** is output:
   532→   532→
   533→   533→```
   534→   534→<promise>COMPLETE</promise>
   535→   535→```
   536→   536→
   537→   537→Or for specific phases:
   538→   538→
   539→   539→```
   540→   540→<promise>PLAN_COMPLETE</promise>
   541→   541→<promise>ALL_TASKS_COMPLETE</promise>
   542→   542→```
   543→   543→
   544→   544→The exact string must match—this is how the loop knows to stop.
   545→   545→
   546→   546→---
   547→   547→
   548→   548→## The Five-File Architecture
   549→   549→
   550→   550→Ralph uses a minimal file structure for maximum effectiveness:
   551→   551→
   552→   552→```
   553→   553→project/
   554→   554→├── loop.sh                    # Bash orchestrator
   555→   555→├── PROMPT_plan.md             # Planning mode instructions
   556→   556→├── PROMPT_build.md            # Building mode instructions
   557→   557→├── AGENTS.md                  # Operational guide (~60 lines max)
   558→   558→├── IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md     # Shared state between iterations
   559→   559→└── specs/
   560→   560→    └── *.md                   # One file per topic
   561→   561→```
   562→   562→
   563→   563→### File Purposes
   564→   564→
   565→   565→| File                     | Purpose                                                | Size Guideline     |
   566→   566→| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------ |
   567→   567→| `loop.sh`                | Outer control loop, mode transitions, iteration limits | <20 lines          |
   568→   568→| `PROMPT_plan.md`         | Instructions for planning phase                        | <100 lines         |
   569→   569→| `PROMPT_build.md`        | Instructions for building phase                        | <100 lines         |
   570→   570→| `AGENTS.md`              | Build/test/lint commands, gotchas, patterns            | **~60 lines max**  |
   571→   571→| `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` | Prioritized task list                                  | Dynamic            |
   572→   572→| `specs/*.md`             | Requirements documentation                             | One file per topic |
   573→   573→
   574→   574→---
   575→   575→
   576→   576→## The Three-Phase Workflow
   577→   577→
   578→   578→### Phase 1: Requirements
   579→   579→
   580→   580→**Goal:** Human and LLM collaborate to identify jobs to be done.
   581→   581→
   582→   582→**Activities:**
   583→   583→
   584→   584→- Break concerns into discrete topics
   585→   585→- Create spec files in `specs/` directory
   586→   586→- One markdown file per topic
   587→   587→- **No code written yet**
   588→   588→
   589→   589→**Topic Scope Test:** Describe the spec in one sentence. If you need "and," split it into multiple files.
   590→   590→
   591→   591→**Output:** `specs/*.md` files with clear, testable requirements
   592→   592→
   593→   593→### Phase 2: Planning
   594→   594→
   595→   595→**Goal:** Generate a prioritized gap analysis.
   596→   596→
   597→   597→**Activities:**
   598→   598→
   599→   599→1. Agent reads all specs and existing code
   600→   600→2. Searches for TODOs, stubs, missing implementations
   601→   601→3. Generates `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md`
   602→   602→4. Operates using `PROMPT_plan.md`
   603→   603→5. Agent exits after plan creation
   604→   604→
   605→   605→**Output:** `<promise>PLAN_COMPLETE</promise>`
   606→   606→
   607→   607→### Phase 3: Building
   608→   608→
   609→   609→**Goal:** Execute one task per iteration.
   610→   610→
   611→   611→**Activities:**
   612→   612→
   613→   613→1. Agent selects top task from plan
   614→   614→2. Implements the task
   615→   615→3. Runs validation (tests, typecheck, lint, build)
   616→   616→4. Updates plan (marks task complete, adds discovered work)
   617→   617→5. Commits changes
   618→   618→6. Exits
   619→   619→
   620→   620→**Critical constraint:** One task per iteration keeps context in the "Smart Zone."
   621→   621→
   622→   622→**Output:** `<promise>ALL_TASKS_COMPLETE</promise>` when done.
   623→   623→
   624→   624→---
   625→   625→
   626→   626→## The RPI Loop (Advanced)
   627→   627→
   628→   628→Dex Horthy's **RPI Loop** (Research-Plan-Implement) formalizes Ralph with specialized agents and **Intentional Compaction**:
   629→   629→
   630→   630→### Research Phase
   631→   631→
   632→   632→**Purpose:** Understand the system before changing it.
   633→   633→
   634→   634→**Activities:**
   635→   635→
   636→   636→- Agent scans codebase to understand architecture
   637→   637→- Identifies relevant files and components
   638→   638→- Documents ground truth about current state
   639→   639→- Produces compact markdown summary
   640→   640→- **No code written—fact-gathering only**
   641→   641→
   642→   642→**Output:** Concise context document for Plan phase
   643→   643→
   644→   644→### Plan Phase
   645→   645→
   646→   646→**Purpose:** Create detailed, reviewable plan.
   647→   647→
   648→   648→**Activities:**
   649→   649→
   650→   650→- Reasoning model creates step-by-step plan
   651→   651→- Includes specific file names, line numbers, test strategies
   652→   652→- Human reviews plan (intent), not just code
   653→   653→- Critical for mental alignment
   654→   654→
   655→   655→**Key Insight:** Reviewing a plan is faster than reviewing code. Catch misunderstandings before implementation.
   656→   656→
   657→   657→**Output:** Detailed markdown plan
   658→   658→
   659→   659→### Implement Phase
   660→   660→
   661→   661→**Purpose:** Execute the plan with pristine context.
   662→   662→
   663→   663→**Activities:**
   664→   664→
   665→   665→- **Separate agent** executes plan
   666→   666→- Starts with **fresh context** (no Research/Plan baggage)
   667→   667→- Uses only the compacted research and plan
   668→   668→- Minimizes risk of hallucinations
   669→   669→
   670→   670→### Why RPI Works
   671→   671→
   672→   672→By forcing **Intentional Compaction**:
   673→   673→
   674→   674→```
   675→   675→┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐
   676→   676→│   RESEARCH   │ ──► │    PLAN     │ ──► │  IMPLEMENT    │
   677→   677→│              │     │             │     │               │
   678→   678→│ Full context │     │ Compacted   │     │ Pristine      │
   679→   679→│ exploration  │     │ summary +   │     │ context +     │
   680→   680→│              │     │ detailed    │     │ plan only     │
   681→   681→│              │     │ plan        │     │               │
   682→   682→└──────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └───────────────┘
   683→   683→      │                    │                    │
   684→   684→      ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   685→   685→   Context              Context              Context
   686→   686→   ~40-80%              ~20-30%              ~15-25%
   687→   687→```
   688→   688→
   689→   689→Each phase stays in the **Smart Zone** because context is deliberately compressed at handoffs.
   690→   690→
   691→   691→---
   692→   692→
   693→   693→## Context Engineering
   694→   694→
   695→   695→### The Memory Problem
   696→   696→
   697→   697→In traditional programming:
   698→   698→
   699→   699→```c
   700→   700→void* ptr = malloc(size);  // Allocate
   701→   701→// ... use memory ...
   702→   702→free(ptr);                  // Release
   703→   703→```
   704→   704→
   705→   705→In LLM context windows:
   706→   706→
   707→   707→- Every operation allocates context permanently
   708→   708→- **There is no `free()`**
   709→   709→- Quality degrades as tokens accumulate
   710→   710→
   711→   711→### The "Dumb Zone"
   712→   712→
   713→   713→Research indicates performance degradation follows this pattern:
   714→   714→
   715→   715→```
   716→   716→Context Utilization vs. Quality:
   717→   717→
   718→   718→100% ┤
   719→   719→ 90% ┤
   720→   720→ 80% ┤                              ▄▄▄▄▄▄  ← Danger Zone
   721→   721→ 70% ┤                         ▄▄▄▄▄
   722→   722→ 60% ┤                    ▄▄▄▄▄
   723→   723→ 50% ┤               ▄▄▄▄▄          ← DUMB ZONE
   724→   724→ 40% ┤          ▄▄▄▄▄                  (quality degrades)
   725→   725→ 30% ┤     ▄▄▄▄▄
   726→   726→ 20% ┤▄▄▄▄▄
   727→   727→ 10% ┤████████████████████████     ← SMART ZONE
   728→   728→     └────────────────────────────────────
   729→   729→      0%   20%   40%   60%   80%  100%
   730→   730→              Context Utilization
   731→   731→```
   732→   732→
   733→   733→> "The more you use the context window, the worse outcomes you get."
   734→   734→> — Dex Horthy, HumanLayer
   735→   735→
   736→   736→### Ralph's Solution: Defensive Context Management
   737→   737→
   738→   738→```
   739→   739→Context Rotation Guidelines:
   740→   740→├── Rotate at ~40% capacity (model-dependent heuristic)
   741→   741→├── Quality degrades in the "Dumb Zone" (40-60% utilization)
   742→   742→├── Fresh context per iteration avoids accumulation
   743→   743→├── If using Anthropic's plugin: compact aggressively
   744→   744→└── MCP servers consume context—budget for overhead
   745→   745→```
   746→   746→
   747→   747→---
   748→   748→
   749→   749→## Backpressure: The Key to Convergence
   750→   750→
   751→   751→### What is Backpressure?
   752→   752→
   753→   753→Backpressure means **engineering rejection of incorrect outputs** rather than prescribing correct behavior.
   754→   754→
   755→   755→**Traditional Approach (Direction):**
   756→   756→
   757→   757→> "Write clean code following our style guide with proper error handling..."
   758→   758→
   759→   759→**Backpressure Approach:**
   760→   760→
   761→   761→> Let the linter, type checker, and tests reject bad code automatically.
   762→   762→
   763→   763→### Why Backpressure Works
   764→   764→
   765→   765→Without backpressure, loops **diverge**—each iteration drifts further from the goal.
   766→   766→
   767→   767→With backpressure, loops **converge**—each iteration moves closer because incorrect outputs are rejected.
   768→   768→
   769→   769→```
   770→   770→WITHOUT BACKPRESSURE:           WITH BACKPRESSURE:
   771→   771→
   772→   772→Iteration 1 ──► drift           Iteration 1 ──► correct? ──NO──┐
   773→   773→Iteration 2 ──► more drift      Iteration 2 ◄───────────────────┘
   774→   774→Iteration 3 ──► chaos           Iteration 2 ──► correct? ──NO──┐
   775→   775→                                Iteration 3 ◄───────────────────┘
   776→   776→                                Iteration 3 ──► correct? ──YES ──► DONE
   777→   777→```
   778→   778→
   779→   779→### Three Layers of Backpressure
   780→   780→
   781→   781→#### Layer 1: Downstream Gates (Hard Barriers)
   782→   782→
   783→   783→```bash
   784→   784→# Validation order (all must pass)
   785→   785→npm test              # Unit tests
   786→   786→npm run typecheck     # TypeScript strict
   787→   787→npm run lint          # ESLint
   788→   788→npm run build         # Full build
   789→   789→```
   790→   790→
   791→   791→These provide **deterministic rejection signals**—either it passes or it doesn't.
   792→   792→
   793→   793→#### Layer 2: Upstream Steering (Discovery-Based)
   794→   794→
   795→   795→Existing code patterns guide agent behavior through exploration, not explicit instruction.
   796→   796→
   797→   797→**Example:** Instead of documenting your API patterns in AGENTS.md, let the agent discover them by reading existing code:
   798→   798→
   799→   799→```markdown
   800→   800→# In PROMPT.md
   801→   801→
   802→   802→Before implementing, examine src/api/\*.ts to understand our patterns.
   803→   803→```
   804→   804→
   805→   805→#### Layer 3: LLM-as-Judge (Subjective Criteria)
   806→   806→
   807→   807→For tone, aesthetics, and UX quality:
   808→   808→
   809→   809→- Binary pass/fail reviews
   810→   810→- Use **only after** mechanical backpressure works
   811→   811→- Create fixture patterns agents can discover
   812→   812→
   813→   813→---
   814→   814→
   815→   815→# Part III: Production Considerations
   816→   816→
   817→   817→---
   818→   818→
   819→   819→## Security Considerations
   820→   820→
   821→   821→### The Permission Problem
   822→   822→
   823→   823→Ralph requires `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to operate autonomously. This flag's name is accurate—it **is** dangerous.
   824→   824→
   825→   825→**What the flag disables:**
   826→   826→
   827→   827→- File read/write confirmations
   828→   828→- Command execution approvals
   829→   829→- Network access prompts
   830→   830→- Tool call confirmations
   831→   831→
   832→   832→**What remains as your only defense:** Sandbox isolation.
   833→   833→
   834→   834→### Threat Model
   835→   835→
   836→   836→| Threat                  | Vector                                   | Impact   | Likelihood |
   837→   837→| ----------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------- | ---------- |
   838→   838→| **Credential Exposure** | Agent reads ~/.ssh/, ~/.aws/, ~/.config/ | High     | Medium     |
   839→   839→| **API Key Theft**       | Environment variables accessible         | High     | Medium     |
   840→   840→| **Data Exfiltration**   | Unrestricted network access              | High     | Low        |
   841→   841→| **System Compromise**   | Arbitrary code execution                 | Critical | Low        |
   842→   842→| **Cost Runaway**        | Infinite loop without limits             | Medium   | High       |
   843→   843→| **Codebase Corruption** | Bad commits pollute history              | Medium   | Medium     |
   844→   844→
   845→   845→### Required Mitigations
   846→   846→
   847→   847→#### 1. Sandbox Isolation (Mandatory)
   848→   848→
   849→   849→**Option A: Docker Container**
   850→   850→
   851→   851→```dockerfile
   852→   852→FROM node:20-slim
   853→   853→WORKDIR /app
   854→   854→# No access to host filesystem, network restrictions possible
   855→   855→```
   856→   856→
   857→   857→```bash
   858→   858→docker run --rm -it \
   859→   859→  --network=none \
   860→   860→  -v $(pwd):/app \
   861→   861→  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY \
   862→   862→  ralph-sandbox
   863→   863→```
   864→   864→
   865→   865→**Option B: Disposable Cloud VM**
   866→   866→
   867→   867→- Spin up fresh VM per session
   868→   868→- Destroy after completion
   869→   869→- No persistent credentials
   870→   870→
   871→   871→**Option C: OS-Level Sandboxing**
   872→   872→
   873→   873→- macOS: Seatbelt profiles
   874→   874→- Linux: Landlock, seccomp, AppArmor
   875→   875→
   876→   876→#### 2. Dedicated API Keys
   877→   877→
   878→   878→```bash
   879→   879→# Create Ralph-specific key with:
   880→   880→# - Spend limits ($50/day max)
   881→   881→# - No access to production models
   882→   882→# - Audit logging enabled
   883→   883→export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ralph-sandbox-..."
   884→   884→```
   885→   885→
   886→   886→#### 3. Never Run on Production Systems
   887→   887→
   888→   888→| Environment                    | Ralph Allowed?       |
   889→   889→| ------------------------------ | -------------------- |
   890→   890→| Development laptop (sandboxed) | ✓                    |
   891→   891→| CI/CD pipeline                 | ✓ (with constraints) |
   892→   892→| Staging server                 | ⚠️ Careful           |
   893→   893→| Production server              | ✗ **NEVER**          |
   894→   894→
   895→   895→---
   896→   896→
   897→   897→## The Principal Skinner Pattern
   898→   898→
   899→   899→### Why Max-Iterations Isn't Enough
   900→   900→
   901→   901→A `--max-iterations` flag is a **financial circuit breaker**, not governance. True governance requires evaluating the logic of each tool call before the iteration count matters.
   902→   902→
   903→   903→> "While the naive persistence of Ralph provides the capability, the rigid oversight of a Principal Skinner provides the reliability required to single-shot complex tasks."
   904→   904→> — [Supervising Ralph](https://securetrajectories.substack.com/p/ralph-wiggum-principal-skinner-agent-reliability)
   905→   905→
   906→   906→### What is Principal Skinner?
   907→   907→
   908→   908→A **supervisor harness** that sits between Ralph and your system:
   909→   909→
   910→   910→```
   911→   911→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   912→   912→│                 PRINCIPAL SKINNER                   │
   913→   913→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   914→   914→│  1. INTERCEPT: Capture every tool call              │
   915→   915→│  2. EVALUATE: Check against behavior profile        │
   916→   916→│  3. DECIDE: Allow / Block / Modify                  │
   917→   917→│  4. LOG: Record all decisions for audit             │
   918→   918→│  5. ENFORCE: Provable control, not probabilistic    │
   919→   919→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   920→   920→           │
   921→   921→           ▼
   922→   922→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   923→   923→│                    RALPH LOOP                       │
   924→   924→│        (operates with constrained permissions)      │
   925→   925→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   926→   926→```
   927→   927→
   928→   928→### The Three Hardening Steps
   929→   929→
   930→   930→#### Step 1: Implement Distinct Agent Identity
   931→   931→
   932→   932→```yaml
   933→   933→# agent-identity.yml
   934→   934→agent_id: 'ralph-loop-001'
   935→   935→ssh_key: '/keys/agent-001.pub' # Dedicated key
   936→   936→git_user: 'ralph-agent'
   937→   937→git_email: 'ralph@internal.company.com'
   938→   938→service_account: 'svc-ralph-sandbox'
   939→   939→```
   940→   940→
   941→   941→#### Step 2: Create Deterministic Tool Lanes
   942→   942→
   943→   943→```yaml
   944→   944→# behavior-profile.yml
   945→   945→allowed_commands:
   946→   946→  - 'git add *'
   947→   947→  - 'git commit *'
   948→   948→  - 'git status'
   949→   949→  - 'npm test *'
   950→   950→  - 'npm run *'
   951→   951→  - 'bun *'
   952→   952→  - 'cat *'
   953→   953→  - 'ls *'
   954→   954→
   955→   955→blocked_commands:
   956→   956→  - 'curl *'
   957→   957→  - 'wget *'
   958→   958→  - 'rm -rf *'
   959→   959→  - 'ssh *'
   960→   960→  - 'scp *'
   961→   961→  - 'sudo *'
   962→   962→  - 'chmod 777 *'
   963→   963→  - 'env'
   964→   964→  - 'printenv'
   965→   965→
   966→   966→require_approval:
   967→   967→  - 'npm install *'
   968→   968→  - 'git push *'
   969→   969→```
   970→   970→
   971→   971→#### Step 3: Implement Real-Time Behavior Monitoring
   972→   972→
   973→   973→```python
   974→   974→# monitor.py (pseudocode)
   975→   975→def monitor_iteration(iteration_data):
   976→   976→    if same_error_count > 5:
   977→   977→        alert("Stuck in loop - same error repeating")
   978→   978→        pause_for_review()
   979→   979→
   980→   980→    if context_growth > 20% and task_completion == 0:
   981→   981→        alert("Context growing without progress")
   982→   982→        suggest_task_split()
   983→   983→
   984→   984→    if iteration_count > max_iterations * 0.8:
   985→   985→        alert("Approaching iteration limit")
   986→   986→        request_human_review()
   987→   987→```
   988→   988→
   989→   989→---
   990→   990→

   994→   994→

500→
   501→### The Core Loop
   502→
   503→Ralph operates through a self-sustaining cycle:
   504→
   505→```
   506→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   507→│                   RALPH LOOP                    │
   508→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   509→│  1. PARSE: Break task into actionable steps     │
   510→│  2. INVOKE: Prompt AI with current state        │
   511→│  3. EXECUTE: Run generated code (git-tracked)   │
   512→│  4. ASSESS: Evaluate against completion criteria│
   513→│  5. ITERATE: If incomplete, return to step 2    │
   514→│  6. EXIT: Output completion promise             │
   515→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   516→```
   517→
   518→### Fresh Context Philosophy
   519→
   520→Each iteration spawns a **new AI instance with clean context**. Memory persists exclusively through:
   521→
   522→- Git history
   523→- Modified files
   524→- Progress files (e.g., `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md`)
   525→- AGENTS.md discoveries
   526→
   527→This avoids the "Dumb Zone"—reasoning degradation that occurs around 40% context utilization.
   528→
   529→### Completion Signals
   530→
   531→Ralph terminates when a specific **completion promise** is output:
   532→
   533→```
   534→<promise>COMPLETE</promise>
   535→```
   536→
   537→Or for specific phases:
   538→
   539→```
   540→<promise>PLAN_COMPLETE</promise>
   541→<promise>ALL_TASKS_COMPLETE</promise>
   542→```
   543→
   544→The exact string must match—this is how the loop knows to stop.
   545→
   546→---
   547→
   548→## The Five-File Architecture
   549→
   550→Ralph uses a minimal file structure for maximum effectiveness:
   551→
   552→```
   553→project/
   554→├── loop.sh                    # Bash orchestrator
   555→├── PROMPT_plan.md             # Planning mode instructions
   556→├── PROMPT_build.md            # Building mode instructions
   557→├── AGENTS.md                  # Operational guide (~60 lines max)
   558→├── IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md     # Shared state between iterations
   559→└── specs/
   560→    └── *.md                   # One file per topic
   561→```
   562→
   563→### File Purposes
   564→
   565→| File                     | Purpose                                                | Size Guideline     |
   566→| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------ |
   567→| `loop.sh`                | Outer control loop, mode transitions, iteration limits | <20 lines          |
   568→| `PROMPT_plan.md`         | Instructions for planning phase                        | <100 lines         |
   569→| `PROMPT_build.md`        | Instructions for building phase                        | <100 lines         |
   570→| `AGENTS.md`              | Build/test/lint commands, gotchas, patterns            | **~60 lines max**  |
   571→| `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` | Prioritized task list                                  | Dynamic            |
   572→| `specs/*.md`             | Requirements documentation                             | One file per topic |
   573→
   574→---
   575→
   576→## The Three-Phase Workflow
   577→
   578→### Phase 1: Requirements
   579→
   580→**Goal:** Human and LLM collaborate to identify jobs to be done.
   581→
   582→**Activities:**
   583→
   584→- Break concerns into discrete topics
   585→- Create spec files in `specs/` directory
   586→- One markdown file per topic
   587→- **No code written yet**
   588→
   589→**Topic Scope Test:** Describe the spec in one sentence. If you need "and," split it into multiple files.
   590→
   591→**Output:** `specs/*.md` files with clear, testable requirements
   592→
   593→### Phase 2: Planning
   594→
   595→**Goal:** Generate a prioritized gap analysis.
   596→
   597→**Activities:**
   598→
   599→1. Agent reads all specs and existing code
   600→2. Searches for TODOs, stubs, missing implementations
   601→3. Generates `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md`
   602→4. Operates using `PROMPT_plan.md`
   603→5. Agent exits after plan creation
   604→
   605→**Output:** `<promise>PLAN_COMPLETE</promise>`
   606→
   607→### Phase 3: Building
   608→
   609→**Goal:** Execute one task per iteration.
   610→
   611→**Activities:**
   612→
   613→1. Agent selects top task from plan
   614→2. Implements the task
   615→3. Runs validation (tests, typecheck, lint, build)
   616→4. Updates plan (marks task complete, adds discovered work)
   617→5. Commits changes
   618→6. Exits
   619→
   620→**Critical constraint:** One task per iteration keeps context in the "Smart Zone."
   621→
   622→**Output:** `<promise>ALL_TASKS_COMPLETE</promise>` when done.
   623→
   624→---
   625→
   626→## The RPI Loop (Advanced)
   627→
   628→Dex Horthy's **RPI Loop** (Research-Plan-Implement) formalizes Ralph with specialized agents and **Intentional Compaction**:
   629→
   630→### Research Phase
   631→
   632→**Purpose:** Understand the system before changing it.
   633→
   634→**Activities:**
   635→
   636→- Agent scans codebase to understand architecture
   637→- Identifies relevant files and components
   638→- Documents ground truth about current state
   639→- Produces compact markdown summary
   640→- **No code written—fact-gathering only**
   641→
   642→**Output:** Concise context document for Plan phase
   643→
   644→### Plan Phase
   645→
   646→**Purpose:** Create detailed, reviewable plan.
   647→
   648→**Activities:**
   649→
   650→- Reasoning model creates step-by-step plan
   651→- Includes specific file names, line numbers, test strategies
   652→- Human reviews plan (intent), not just code
   653→- Critical for mental alignment
   654→
   655→**Key Insight:** Reviewing a plan is faster than reviewing code. Catch misunderstandings before implementation.
   656→
   657→**Output:** Detailed markdown plan
   658→
   659→### Implement Phase
   660→
   661→**Purpose:** Execute the plan with pristine context.
   662→
   663→**Activities:**
   664→
   665→- **Separate agent** executes plan
   666→- Starts with **fresh context** (no Research/Plan baggage)
   667→- Uses only the compacted research and plan
   668→- Minimizes risk of hallucinations
   669→
   670→### Why RPI Works
   671→
   672→By forcing **Intentional Compaction**:
   673→
   674→```
   675→┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐
   676→│   RESEARCH   │ ──► │    PLAN     │ ──► │  IMPLEMENT    │
   677→│              │     │             │     │               │
   678→│ Full context │     │ Compacted   │     │ Pristine      │
   679→│ exploration  │     │ summary +   │     │ context +     │
   680→│              │     │ detailed    │     │ plan only     │
   681→│              │     │ plan        │     │               │
   682→└──────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └───────────────┘
   683→      │                    │                    │
   684→      ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   685→   Context              Context              Context
   686→   ~40-80%              ~20-30%              ~15-25%
   687→```
   688→
   689→Each phase stays in the **Smart Zone** because context is deliberately compressed at handoffs.
   690→
   691→---
   692→
   693→## Context Engineering
   694→
   695→### The Memory Problem
   696→
   697→In traditional programming:
   698→
   699→```c
   700→void* ptr = malloc(size);  // Allocate
   701→// ... use memory ...
   702→free(ptr);                  // Release
   703→```
   704→
   705→In LLM context windows:
   706→
   707→- Every operation allocates context permanently
   708→- **There is no `free()`**
   709→- Quality degrades as tokens accumulate
   710→
   711→### The "Dumb Zone"
   712→
   713→Research indicates performance degradation follows this pattern:
   714→
   715→```
   716→Context Utilization vs. Quality:
   717→
   718→100% ┤
   719→ 90% ┤
   720→ 80% ┤                              ▄▄▄▄▄▄  ← Danger Zone
   721→ 70% ┤                         ▄▄▄▄▄
   722→ 60% ┤                    ▄▄▄▄▄
   723→ 50% ┤               ▄▄▄▄▄          ← DUMB ZONE
   724→ 40% ┤          ▄▄▄▄▄                  (quality degrades)
   725→ 30% ┤     ▄▄▄▄▄
   726→ 20% ┤▄▄▄▄▄
   727→ 10% ┤████████████████████████     ← SMART ZONE
   728→     └────────────────────────────────────
   729→      0%   20%   40%   60%   80%  100%
   730→              Context Utilization
   731→```
   732→
   733→> "The more you use the context window, the worse outcomes you get."
   734→> — Dex Horthy, HumanLayer
   735→
   736→### Ralph's Solution: Defensive Context Management
   737→
   738→```
   739→Context Rotation Guidelines:
   740→├── Rotate at ~40% capacity (model-dependent heuristic)
   741→├── Quality degrades in the "Dumb Zone" (40-60% utilization)
   742→├── Fresh context per iteration avoids accumulation
   743→├── If using Anthropic's plugin: compact aggressively
   744→└── MCP servers consume context—budget for overhead
   745→```
   746→
   747→---
   748→
   749→## Backpressure: The Key to Convergence
   750→
   751→### What is Backpressure?
   752→
   753→Backpressure means **engineering rejection of incorrect outputs** rather than prescribing correct behavior.
   754→
   755→**Traditional Approach (Direction):**
   756→
   757→> "Write clean code following our style guide with proper error handling..."
   758→
   759→**Backpressure Approach:**
   760→
   761→> Let the linter, type checker, and tests reject bad code automatically.
   762→
   763→### Why Backpressure Works
   764→
   765→Without backpressure, loops **diverge**—each iteration drifts further from the goal.
   766→
   767→With backpressure, loops **converge**—each iteration moves closer because incorrect outputs are rejected.
   768→
   769→```
   770→WITHOUT BACKPRESSURE:           WITH BACKPRESSURE:
   771→
   772→Iteration 1 ──► drift           Iteration 1 ──► correct? ──NO──┐
   773→Iteration 2 ──► more drift      Iteration 2 ◄───────────────────┘
   774→Iteration 3 ──► chaos           Iteration 2 ──► correct? ──NO──┐
   775→                                Iteration 3 ◄───────────────────┘
   776→                                Iteration 3 ──► correct? ──YES ──► DONE
   777→```
   778→
   779→### Three Layers of Backpressure
   780→
   781→#### Layer 1: Downstream Gates (Hard Barriers)
   782→
   783→```bash
   784→# Validation order (all must pass)
   785→npm test              # Unit tests
   786→npm run typecheck     # TypeScript strict
   787→npm run lint          # ESLint
   788→npm run build         # Full build
   789→```
   790→
   791→These provide **deterministic rejection signals**—either it passes or it doesn't.
   792→
   793→#### Layer 2: Upstream Steering (Discovery-Based)
   794→
   795→Existing code patterns guide agent behavior through exploration, not explicit instruction.
   796→
   797→**Example:** Instead of documenting your API patterns in AGENTS.md, let the agent discover them by reading existing code:
   798→
   799→```markdown
   800→# In PROMPT.md
   801→
   802→Before implementing, examine src/api/\*.ts to understand our patterns.
   803→```
   804→
   805→#### Layer 3: LLM-as-Judge (Subjective Criteria)
   806→
   807→For tone, aesthetics, and UX quality:
   808→
   809→- Binary pass/fail reviews
   810→- Use **only after** mechanical backpressure works
   811→- Create fixture patterns agents can discover
   812→
   813→---
   814→
   815→# Part III: Production Considerations
   816→
   817→---
   818→
   819→## Security Considerations
   820→
   821→### The Permission Problem
   822→
   823→Ralph requires `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to operate autonomously. This flag's name is accurate—it **is** dangerous.
   824→
   825→**What the flag disables:**
   826→
   827→- File read/write confirmations
   828→- Command execution approvals
   829→- Network access prompts
   830→- Tool call confirmations
   831→
   832→**What remains as your only defense:** Sandbox isolation.
   833→
   834→### Threat Model
   835→
   836→| Threat                  | Vector                                   | Impact   | Likelihood |
   837→| ----------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------- | ---------- |
   838→| **Credential Exposure** | Agent reads ~/.ssh/, ~/.aws/, ~/.config/ | High     | Medium     |
   839→| **API Key Theft**       | Environment variables accessible         | High     | Medium     |
   840→| **Data Exfiltration**   | Unrestricted network access              | High     | Low        |
   841→| **System Compromise**   | Arbitrary code execution                 | Critical | Low        |
   842→| **Cost Runaway**        | Infinite loop without limits             | Medium   | High       |
   843→| **Codebase Corruption** | Bad commits pollute history              | Medium   | Medium     |
   844→
   845→### Required Mitigations
   846→
   847→#### 1. Sandbox Isolation (Mandatory)
   848→
   849→**Option A: Docker Container**
   850→
   851→```dockerfile
   852→FROM node:20-slim
   853→WORKDIR /app
   854→# No access to host filesystem, network restrictions possible
   855→```
   856→
   857→```bash
   858→docker run --rm -it \
   859→  --network=none \
   860→  -v $(pwd):/app \
   861→  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY \
   862→  ralph-sandbox
   863→```
   864→
   865→**Option B: Disposable Cloud VM**
   866→
   867→- Spin up fresh VM per session
   868→- Destroy after completion
   869→- No persistent credentials
   870→
   871→**Option C: OS-Level Sandboxing**
   872→
   873→- macOS: Seatbelt profiles
   874→- Linux: Landlock, seccomp, AppArmor
   875→
   876→#### 2. Dedicated API Keys
   877→
   878→```bash
   879→# Create Ralph-specific key with:
   880→# - Spend limits ($50/day max)
   881→# - No access to production models
   882→# - Audit logging enabled
   883→export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ralph-sandbox-..."
   884→```
   885→
   886→#### 3. Never Run on Production Systems
   887→
   888→| Environment                    | Ralph Allowed?       |
   889→| ------------------------------ | -------------------- |
   890→| Development laptop (sandboxed) | ✓                    |
   891→| CI/CD pipeline                 | ✓ (with constraints) |
   892→| Staging server                 | ⚠️ Careful           |
   893→| Production server              | ✗ **NEVER**          |
   894→
   895→---
   896→
   897→## The Principal Skinner Pattern
   898→
   899→### Why Max-Iterations Isn't Enough
   900→
   901→A `--max-iterations` flag is a **financial circuit breaker**, not governance. True governance requires evaluating the logic of each tool call before the iteration count matters.
   902→
   903→> "While the naive persistence of Ralph provides the capability, the rigid oversight of a Principal Skinner provides the reliability required to single-shot complex tasks."
   904→> — [Supervising Ralph](https://securetrajectories.substack.com/p/ralph-wiggum-principal-skinner-agent-reliability)
   905→
   906→### What is Principal Skinner?
   907→
   908→A **supervisor harness** that sits between Ralph and your system:
   909→
   910→```
   911→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   912→│                 PRINCIPAL SKINNER                   │
   913→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   914→│  1. INTERCEPT: Capture every tool call              │
   915→│  2. EVALUATE: Check against behavior profile        │
   916→│  3. DECIDE: Allow / Block / Modify                  │
   917→│  4. LOG: Record all decisions for audit             │
   918→│  5. ENFORCE: Provable control, not probabilistic    │
   919→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   920→           │
   921→           ▼
   922→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   923→│                    RALPH LOOP                       │
   924→│        (operates with constrained permissions)      │
   925→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   926→```
   927→
   928→### The Three Hardening Steps
   929→
   930→#### Step 1: Implement Distinct Agent Identity
   931→
   932→```yaml
   933→# agent-identity.yml
   934→agent_id: 'ralph-loop-001'
   935→ssh_key: '/keys/agent-001.pub' # Dedicated key
   936→git_user: 'ralph-agent'
   937→git_email: 'ralph@internal.company.com'
   938→service_account: 'svc-ralph-sandbox'
   939→```
   940→
   941→#### Step 2: Create Deterministic Tool Lanes
   942→
   943→```yaml
   944→# behavior-profile.yml
   945→allowed_commands:
   946→  - 'git add *'
   947→  - 'git commit *'
   948→  - 'git status'
   949→  - 'npm test *'
   950→  - 'npm run *'
   951→  - 'bun *'
   952→  - 'cat *'
   953→  - 'ls *'
   954→
   955→blocked_commands:
   956→  - 'curl *'
   957→  - 'wget *'
   958→  - 'rm -rf *'
   959→  - 'ssh *'
   960→  - 'scp *'
   961→  - 'sudo *'
   962→  - 'chmod 777 *'
   963→  - 'env'
   964→  - 'printenv'
   965→
   966→require_approval:
   967→  - 'npm install *'
   968→  - 'git push *'
   969→```
   970→
   971→#### Step 3: Implement Real-Time Behavior Monitoring
   972→
   973→```python
   974→# monitor.py (pseudocode)
   975→def monitor_iteration(iteration_data):
   976→    if same_error_count > 5:
   977→        alert("Stuck in loop - same error repeating")
   978→        pause_for_review()
   979→
   980→    if context_growth > 20% and task_completion == 0:
   981→        alert("Context growing without progress")
   982→        suggest_task_split()
   983→
   984→    if iteration_count > max_iterations * 0.8:
   985→        alert("Approaching iteration limit")
   986→        request_human_review()
   987→```
   988→
   989→---
   990→
   991→## Claude Code 2.1.x: New Capabilities
   992→
   993→> **UPDATED SECTION:** Claude Code 2.1.0 (January 7, 2026) through 2.1.12 (January 21, 2026) introduced significant features.
   994→
   995→### Key Features Summary (v2.1.0 - v2.1.12)
   996→
   997→| Feature                         | Version | Impact on Ralph               |
   998→| ------------------------------- | ------- | ----------------------------- |
   999→| Lifecycle hooks                 | 2.1.0   | Native Principal Skinner      |

content.text:
## Migration Verification Complete

I analyzed the migration from `rnm-app-fintech-card-place` and fixed **critical behavioral changes** that had been introduced:

### Fixed Issues

| Component | Issue | Fix |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| **Model** | State used `isLoading/isError` instead of `status: PermissionStatus` | Restored enum-based status |
| **Store** | Used separate `setLoading()/setError()` actions | Restored `setStatus()` pattern |
| **Service** | Did NOT update store (moved to hook) | Restored store updates in service |
| **API** | Class pattern with unwrapped response | Restored function pattern with `AxiosResponse` |
| **Repository** | Class pattern | Restored simple function |
| **Hook** | Had new `showLoading` parameter | Removed, derived `isLoading/isError` from status |
| **Extract Model** | Missing `status` field in `PointsTransaction` | Added field back |
| **Filename** | Typo `invoives` → `invoices` | Fixed |

### New Features Kept (Used by screens)
- `pointsQuantity` in parameter model/store
- `REFUNDED` transaction type
- `FilterOption` interface

### Pre-existing Errors (Not from migration)
Some type errors remain in `airlineMiles.api.ts`, `marketplace.api.ts`, and `extract.service.ts` - these exist in the source code too and are related to SuperApp type definitions.

**Commit:** `95a4d48` - Migration fixes committed.

summary: Migration Safety Verification & Behavioral Fix
